title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lucy strolls through the gardens of Laceford Manor, noting the well-kept vegetable patch near the house, which she attributes to Emma. She walks along a driveway, passes under an iron bridge arch near the railway track, and enters a narrow backstreet bordered by a railway embankment and a factory wall. At a local post office, Lucy uses a phone booth to call a woman, identifying herself as Miss Esperanza. She informs the woman that she has arrived safely and will report any new discoveries, requesting that she not disturb the recipient, who is resting.

Back at the manor, Lucy practices golf on the lawn. During a conversation, Old Mr. Crickenshaw expresses his refusal to die so that others can build houses on his land. He voices suspicions regarding Cedric, Harold, and Alfred, noting that his illness is mysterious and even Dr. Campbell could not determine its cause. 

The following day, while searching for a golf ball on the railway embankment, Lucy discovers a broken thorny scrub. She finds a piece of light brown fur caught on a thorn and a cheap porcelain powder box among the weeds. She also observes blurred, old footprints in the grass.

Lucy visits her aunt, Miss Marple, at Madison No. 4. Lucy presents the fur and the powder box, explaining how she found them. Miss Marple suggests the items fell from a woman in a light fur coat after her body was moved down the embankment. Miss Marple argues that the evidence supports a hypothesis that the crime was a murder, rather than the body simply being lost, suggesting the killer moved the body from the railway.
